content: Prepare to get bored,   I am currently sited outside my crib typing my fingers away despite being as hungry as every sense of the word comes. I have a roommate ( or is it roommates?), who has not bothered to know what I have eaten for the past fortnight. I am prepared to bore my readers due to my indisposition to exertion on matters concerning this blog. No. I don’t want to bore you all about how my creative juices have become diluted, how I have a writer’s mental block or any philosophical blabber towards such. No. I will not do injustice to the few readers who walk into this space with a thirst in their guts like a virgin on her wedding night. I know you are bored already but bear a little. I am giving up. No, not on the blog (or not yet?). I am giving up on a lot of other things. An effort to not laugh when people I hate make really nice dry jokes. I am an enthusiast of the driest of jokes. Jokes so dehydrated, joke so bare and lacking moist they could be Sahara desert’s bastard. I am giving up on holding shit inside when I’m pressed. (Okay, I got to rush inside and have a quick dump. Bear with me. Listen to Sia when I’m gone or something.) Aaaaaah. That was great. Taking a good dump is one of the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit. Right above ummmh….what were they?? Anyone? I really love this blog. I love her. I need a name for her. My lips curve in excitement when I think of the beautiful stories she makes me tell her. I blush when she stares at me when I don’t have stories to tell her. At such moment, we listen to some good music (Most of the times Sia calms her soul). It is at such times that she stares across the laptop from me while she tears up telling me of her lonely days. Of days I have abandoned her for Calculus. Or Java. Or physics. It is at such times that I hold out my hands and feel hers and assure her that I am just friends with science. She cries and says I lie. She has seen the texts. All the notes I have on the Laws of Thermodynamics. I feared it. She says she has seen all the PDFs I have stored in my PC. “You are my one true love,” I assure her in between her sobs. I tell her how all the laws of the universe and how math and science and techn….But she cuts me short by placing her index finger on my lips. “I want to hear about your bad days, not about your friends that I am jealous of,” she whispers. She still maintains her beautiful face when she cries. It’s my turn to cry now. I tell her of my father, my family, my sick aunt who has been in hospital for close to a year. I tell her of the days I cried sited at a bench in a place I will not disclose. All the while she is silent. I tell her also, of the beautiful thing I have thought of the two of us. How far we have come. How much we need a vacation. But money seems to elude me. How I want us to completely move out of this ghetto and have our own beautiful place. A place we could tell the world even greater stories. But money seems to elude me. “Where do you want us to go?” She asks I look far past her across the laptop and squint. I am in deep thought. “Domain baby. Domain.” I say. A new tear forms in her eyes. “What will you call me now. I have been waiting for the name for a long time since we moved in together.” She asks with great concern. It’s a question that has pestered me for not short a while also. “I will tell you when I get more creative about it,” I answer. “Just don’t cheat on me with science,” She says with great concern. I cup her face in the laptop and give her a goodnight kiss as I kick the Calculus book with a heel way back under the bed.

content: Just below snakes and enclosed spaces, street bullies( street kids?) scare the shit out of me. I don’t know how to put this in a nice way so as not to seem as if I am talking from a point of privilege. But it’s true, street kids scare last night’s dinner out of my colons. And if I happen to take porridge for supper, then they don’t even have to try to scare it out. They are right there above my fear of mutura, so you should know it is pretty serious. Therefore when I walk in town I tend to keep myself. Dodging mutura stalls and street kids or I will have to resort to carrying diapers. Nasty lifestyle. I go to town only when it is absolutely necessary. And absolute is absolute, you can never argue with Mathematics; it is the language of the gods after all. When you have to meet me, please make sure it is not in town. We can meet in a church, a jungle, a bar, a strip club, our houses, heaven, but not in town goddamit! The probability of me being bullied by a street kid is 1, and that’s absolute. Let’s also not meet at a mutura stall, because that stuff is shaped like intestines, which therefore does not do me good by reminding me of the part of my body that is scared shitless when I am bullied by a street kid. Do not also organize a meeting at a snake park. Not even a snake graveyard, if there is one. I don’t trust those things even when they are dead. They made Eve eat the apple at the Garden of Eden and we have never stopped since then. You can’t trust something like that. Also, apples are very expensive. Nairobi scares me the most, and then all other towns cram for second place. I think Kisii street kids scare me the least. Or not at all even. I used to think it’s because I cannot get scared in my own mother tongue but then I remembered that is the language my mother uses. If a Kisii mother wants to whoop the hell out of you just stay still and cover your face. If you run away make sure you carry along a hoe. No, no, no. not that type. Carry a hoe to help you dig your own grave. Because threats in a Kisii tongue are not bluffs. If your neighbor is a Kisii and you have an altercation and then they suddenly switch to Kisii; you better apologize even if you are on the wrong. (The next paragraphs were written two hours later because Khaligraph came on TV while I was typing. You garra respect the OG. Let’s not even argue.) Where were we? Ooh, yes. If it is necessary to meet me any town, make sure it is in Kisii Town. But does the Magunga online bookstore know that? Your guess is not as good as mine. Okay, it is. They don’t! When I won a book from the Storymoja Festival, I received a call only a snake park owner would want to torture me with. “Hello, is this Mr. Richard?” First of all, I am 18 years of age; Mr. is kind of overreaching. “This is he, how may I help you?” “I am calling on behalf of the Magunga bookstores to organize for you to pick your book from town at a time of your choice.” “Will you carry diapers for me?” Okay, I did not ask that last one. A few things to note: I had really hoped it would be Magunga himself who would be making that delivery. Or at least the call. You read a man’s blog like it’s your bible but he cannot make a call when you need it. Ja Karuoth yawa! I cannot wait to be famous like that. It comes with its own standards. Again, there will never, ever ever, be a time of my choice to meet in Nairobi town. Unless you plan to fly my ass all the way, and back. My life has been threatened not a few times in this town. So no thank you there is no time called ‘time of my choice’. I rechecked the title of the book. I crossed my fingers and hoped it would read something like, “How to change your diapers in the midst of town”.Damn! It was not. The book was called “Tuesday” by John Elnathan. From what I heard, he and Magunga had met in town to eat fish. The audacity! They make it look so easy this meeting it town business. It’s like…like going to a strip club. So there was no way his book had prepared me for street kids. He does not know the struggle.   “Are you there?” The voice at the other end brought me back from my thoughts. “Tell Magunga I am scared.” “Huh?” “Is there a way you could just deliver it to Ruiru?” My stomach started doing back flips. “Well yes, but I was of the opinion that Town is convenient to both of us.” “Well, you can eat your opinion for supper then.” I muttered under my breath. Silence. Silence. (Meanwhile, Safaricom is chopping moni at the other side.) “Let me see what I can do about it then call you again.” He says before boycotting the call. Shit! I don’t have diapers in this house.

content: I might have to bid the city goodbye and go back to my village because there is a cycle of unprecedented events there, My village Chief started small like it was a joke. He said water that swept downhill to his farm had carried weed seeds. Weed is meant to be uprooted. But we never realized his was a different kind till youths started flocking his compound. It is not peculiar for youths to be in a Chief’s compound frequently. But when your elder brother impregnates the village chief’s eldest daughter, the girl with two left buttocks who was found taking a dump at the church podium on a fine Sunday morning, then shit has hit the fan. (Pun intended). When you hear that your village chief is now a bhang peddler, you garra go because the whole village depends on your academic prowess to solve this issue. They do not give a mad girl’s ass whether you are studying Anthropology or Economics in campus; they raised you to learn the entire world’s politics. I might have to leave this toxic city and pack for the village soon because my father is troubled, My crack-head brother was on his way from the river with our flock of cattle when he spotted Nyanchama, the two left buttocks girl. The geography of the village is that the path to the river stream passes right outside the chief’s compound. His senses must have seeped right through his porous pothead and he forgot about the cows. It so happens that the weed farm is right next to the path where he was. The cows had a feast while his hands were exploring the geography of the two left buttocks girl. As we speak, the village chief wants compensation for the damage on his bhang. As we speak, my father has already sent word to the chief telling him in no uncertain terms will he be summoned by a bhang farmer and if he feels offended he should go sit on a hot jiko. I might have to go to the village immediately because scenes are turning ugly, My father who owns the sole butchery in the village sold weed-meat to church members who are having a camp meeting in the village. The cow that he had slaughtered is among the ones that fed on the Chief’s weed farm. My village members do not care whether I study Computer Science or English Literature; I have to be a Forensic Scientist and solve this matter. Now the pastor is said to be quoting severally from the burning bush scene in the bible. The choir members are all singing about Mt Zion. They are talking of dreams of brighter days, and aliens , and possible ramifications of existence of parallel universes. The weed has taken effect.   I might have to go to my village this moment because events have escalated real quick, After dealing with munchies and eating almost all of the week’s food that they had carried from the camp in one day, the church members must have had a lot of energy to release. Now, it so happens that the ground they were camping at is just uphill from the Chief’s farm. It also so happens that this is coincidentally where the water that brought weed seeds to the Chief’s farm allegedly came from. The Church members with their newly pumped energy picked hoes, I mean a lot of hoes, hoes of all sizes and shapes and attitude. They also carried other farm tools but the hoes were the most. They set down the campsite and decided to do volunteer work. My village members do not care what I study at campus, but they think I should know theology and come and help sorting the crisis. I might have to go to the village because reports reaching me minutes ago are scary.   The church members are uprooting everything in every farm they come across. The first one right next to the campsite is the Chief’s. The Chief is sharpening his panga while cursing. My father sips busaa and laughs hard as he tells me this on the phone. Seconds later I receive a text from my crackhead brother: “Bro, I accidently dropped a blunt in Dad’s busaa pot. The same one he was drinking from when he called you. I am afraid to tell him.” Bruuuuuuuuh. I garra go. My village needs me!

content: 10:45pm on Sameta Hills “Can you imagine how many people are making love at this very moment in all these houses?” I ask her She turns her face abruptly towards me and stares as if she has just found the man who betrayed the great Sakawa. “Going by the fact that it is a little before midnight, I’d have to say it’s more than half the huts.” She replies. “I would hate to be that guy that has to be unlucky tonight. In a night like this when the moon is kissing the stars and the blush from the pink of their billion faces is bespoke. I would hate to be that guy that quarreled with the wife and now their backs have to lie facing each other. Or that one who ate his wife’s avocado that she had so much looked forward to and now the tips of their buttocks is the best contact they have. Or the one who is polygamous and got too drunk he forgot which hut he was to pleasure his groins from.” I say subconsciously. “What?” I am losing her in the train of thoughts. “I am just happy I am not the unlucky guy. I mean, here I am on top of a hill with the love of my life being slapped by a biting breeze while watching the village sleep like it’s a strange new dog.” I smile. “Well guess what?” She turns to me yet again. “Sakawa is not really dead and 911 was an inside job?” I spew a chuckle “You’re such a hollow person.” She replies as she starts off down the hill “Come on, I was only pulling your broomstick!” I shout and pick my Chief’s staff to run after her. I take one look at the village from up here. Tonight I be the lucky guy, tomorrow I begin my rule. Footnote: Pulling your broomstick is potterhead for pulling your leg. I am the newly crowned Village Chief. Also, Chapati is overrated. Did I say Chapati is overrated? Yes. Chapati is overrated.

content: I.BEFORE It would be one week before Christmas when everything starts getting heated. You remember the previous year and how events were awesome beyond your expectation. But currently as you sit outside your hut swinging that metal cup of porridge in your hand to moderate its temperature for the sake of your fourteen year old mouth, you muse upon your broke status. Your mama arrives with a container full of cow dung and starts the process of mixing it with very yellow loam soil. Your houses are only smeared around this time of the year. Aaaarh! The smell of wet soil always gives you an appetite for something your mind cannot quite place. So you leave and allow mama to pimp he hut.  An overly overrated fruit called avocado falls from the tree outside the house as you walk away. “Mama, is this ripe?” You ask as you bend to pick the overrated fruit. (Okay, I think you all get my point) “No. It just fell to impress you handsome.”  She strikes you with a low blow without even looking up from her work. As you strut your ass away you remember last year when you almost got lucky with Nyaboke, that voluptuous lass from across the road that every lad drools for. Rumors are that her grandfather’s grandfather was a cousin to your great grand aunt’s in-law from Nyamira. But in the moment of heat that Christmas night you were legally blind you could not look that far up the lineage tree. You were so close in fact, were it not for that untimely cane that pierced your young buttocks making your manhood recoil inwards with such velocity you decided never to waste your money going to a tortoise park. Nyaboke’s bastard older brother had been passing by the bush when he heard your sounds in the bush humping and preparing for a romp. The speed that got you off a thorough whooping by that douche bag could make your mama proud but in totally unrelated circumstances of course.  Like during night-running sessions. Because that’s what you city folks think we always do down here! You people must really think that when a Kisii kid gets to about ten years of age they receive a Hogwarts letter to join witchcraft. By the way if that actually happened it would be so cool I would not even think twice about joining. But I digress. At such a time before Christmas everyone is looking to solicit gifts from their parents or relatives from the great Nairobi who will be arriving in a few days. But you know you will get nothing from them apart from their annoying and attention seeking spoilt kids you call cousins. They come here speaking sheng in their clean tongues and it is only a million times you’ve held yourself back from punching  their smug faces down into their throats. Their parents are no different; promising you shit they always never deliver. This year, your aunt with a shrilled voice promised you a bicycle. Okay, that aunt who has one eyebrow because all of your aunts have shrilled voices anyway. Or it’s just your eardrums that have a knack of filtering the gutter that comes out of their small mouths into shrieking sounds. It’s an awesome thing that your ears can do. You feel like a god. It is way better that being promised a bicycle that will not get delivered because of three reasons. One, you do not know how to ride a bike because when your age mates used to saddle bicycles with their small groins you were busy doing better things like thirsting for that voluptuous lass Nyaboke. Two, you really have no interest in receiving gifts from city dwelling aunts who always want to talk to you in English. Something about that bugs the sauce in you.  As for the third reason,I really have no interest of telling you baggers. You must be really miserable if you want to hear it. The chime of coins from near bushes gets your adrenaline rushing: its korondo season. Korondo for you spoilt city folks is a money game where one hides coins in their palm in a random order and you place your arrangement next to it for prediction. You earn your correct prediction and lose the unlucky ones.  Because it’s a few days before Christmas, every lad is huddled in the bushes playing korondo like nonsense. You are not to be left behind. You reach into the back pocket of your shorts and your hand touches your ass in the cowboy underwear. Your heart skips a bit. Your pocket is torn. Your coins are not there. You stop and check all your front pockets and luckily your coins are safe. It took a great deal to steal them; you cannot afford to lose them. Yeah, stolen. I wonder where y’all fancy folks expect a fourteen year old brat to get his money from. Custom and tradition prevent a circumcised boy from stepping into their mother’s sleeping chambers. But screw traditions, right? You remember the ordeal last year when you had tiptoed into your mother’s den to gather some coins and came out to sit under the avocado tree like the pretentious little crap that you are. Your friends and your mother had just arrived in the compound. In your escape from the bedroom, your mama’s mothers- union knicker had gotten stuck on your head but you didn’t realize so. The laughter from those scoundrels  as your mama whoop your ass still echoes in your head most nights. She does not negotiate with terrorists that one. “That woman needs a leash.” You think as you run your palm on your head to double-check and then step into a korondo den. II.THE EVE At around six in the evening most if not all households have prepared dough for mandazi or chapati. It is the only time of the year that you will get to eat such delicacies. If you are lucky enough, some meat will be prepared for supper and so you will walk with some raw pepper in your pocket as you prepare for that butchery product. From my thorough research which is sponsored by Hogwarts Department of Festival Wizardry, I have come to realize that on the eve of Christmas Witches do not ride their brooms across the Muggle villages of Kisii. Fear the power of a pregnant virgin woman! They cannot possibly wand their way through such a phenomenon. So when night ushers in darkness on the 24th of December, children come out to ululate. All across the huts in the village, drums will be mercilessly beaten with no particular rhythm as children sing on-spot composed songs. One will shout from across the valley how their Christmas chicken is currently boiling. The other from across will brag of how they have had a taste of busaa. It is only during these periods that children are allowed to have a taste of that sweet drink. They will sing and shout through the night till their voices are sore.  Let it be known that I matured from these children behavior and as the adult I am I stopped doing it long time ago when I was eighteen years. I am nineteen now . Most of the times, the children will go around households and get a piece of whatever is being cooked in each hut. This is like the way whites give candy to children singing carols. Only there are no carols in Kisii and definitely no candy. We are too busy training for inter-village Witchcraft World Cup for that. It is around this time last year that you almost got lucky with Nyaboke. So you leave adults in the house to beat stories as you head out to look for her at midnight among the flock of children singing and shouting at the roads and village paths. If you know where her bastard brother is then you might just get lucky. If you don’t know where he might emerge from, you better carry a charm for protection. For the night is dark and full of terrors. III. CHRISTMAS You will wake up and slip into your new T-shirt and jeans attire that was purposefully bought for Christmas. Your old sports shoes were well wiped and kept since a month ago. You slide into the Reeboks. It doesn’t matter whether they are Nike or Puma or Jordan, all sports shoes are called Reeboks in Kisii. After gobbling your breakfast and confirming your hundred shilling note in your pocket, you head out to town. The movie den is open by that time and you cannot even fathom how many Dj Afro movies you will watch with your hundred shillings. You look up and see a plane in the clouds. Someone has probably packed for Seychelles, or the Kenyan coast, or Europe with their family for the holidays. You in your cheap new attire and hundred shillings have no idea about this and would really care less if you did. Because in your Reeboks, and hundred shillings, and a night of shouting and singing, you feel like you had the best Christmas period on earth. The virtue of simplicity is the supreme haven of happiness! Have a Reebok Christmas gang!

content: ‘The greatness of any nation lies in its fidelity to the constitution, adherence to the rule of law, respect to God and above all the love for mutura,” Chief Justice David Maraga 2017 AD, The Year of the Lord. If you want to treat me to a good meal in this economy, do not take me to a restaurant where I have to wait for an hour to be served something that does not even cover a quarter of my plate. Do not take me to those overrated places in town where I am offered a  tea spoon, a regular spoon, fork, knife, a machete and an axe before food is brought to the table. At that level, the only meal I would expect served is a briefcase from the NYS loot. My Gusii self even gets troubled when I have to tie a piece of handkerchief on my neck( I was later informed it is called a napkin). All this trouble for you to only bring me a slice of chapati and an avocado seed. It reminds me of a time I lied to a voluptuous lass called Nyaboke for months on end about how strong my “bed-minton” game is. When I was finally given a chance to tour the coveted territories, I was done before she could say, “gaaki!” Those are not fun times to make a lad remember. So if you want me to win the hunger games take me to a kibanda, or where us who come from the upper and fertile region of River Gucha call Villa Rosa Kibandaski.( Nyamira people are not allowed to say that; get your pronunciation from Matiang’i.)In those days of the year when I am extremely broke, I take my meals from a Kibandaski; and for someone whose streak in broke days is unbeaten, that is saying a lot. The Kibanda has always been a place of mystery due to its unwritten codes of conduct.  The most important one is what has helped me survive my recent escapades as a newbie mutura enthusiast; Never be curious about the preparation process. When I first dragged my village ass to the city, I was dropped at a place in downtown called Nyamakima which translates to “A person of Ugali.”  I walked around tentatively expecting to see a kibanda that sells Ugali. Imagine the thorough disappointment when the only thing I saw was several barbeque stands that sold what I later came to know as mutura. Its resemblance bordered between a roast tapeworm and charred intestines of an elephant ailing from Obororo; a disease that terrorizes chicken in my village. Chicken that suffer from Obororo are known to pee a lot. Your city ass has not seen a chicken pee in your life but I have; so trust the terror that was the mutura first impression. Have you ever asked yourself a question like, “Self, what the heck is happening here?!” That was my consistent reaction when I saw people from all crawls of life huddled over a chopping board along the streets, devouring something so vile I considered it to be a burnt sacrifice to the Shit gods. I mean, how do you eat something that is possibly colon that has been stuffed with excrement? I had to check my high school Biology to confirm that large intestines are right above that word Trump used on African countries. I also figured out longtime ago that the reason as to why mutura is sold in the evening hours was to allow everyone to have diarrhea at the comfort of their houses at night.  There was no way I would partake in that nonsense. I confirmed I was the sane one. Well, at least until last week. You see, we all have one life to live and no one has confirmed what happens to us on the other side after our air supply is cut off. On Monday last week, I got to deep thoughts about life after death. What if after I die God asks you, “What did you do with the mutura that I gave you?” I would stutter for the answer. In the corner of my eye, I would see Jesus producing a master key for my special room in purgatory . Mbugua, the mutura guy would be the one handling my torture in his white lab coat that has now turned brown with stains of large intestine juices. “I had thought I should trust my gut instincts when around those pieces of guts,” I would answer the almighty. Angel Gabriel giggles because he is the only when who saw what I did there. Cool guy that one. “Oooh, Jesus Christ! Who told you to do that?” God shouts . Looking up from the key-duplicating machine surprised, Jesus answers, “ummmmh, You did.” “Aaah. Sorry son, I’m not talking to you,” God would reply, face to his palm. Gabriel chuckles. I stifle mine and fist pump him real quick. But I digress. Where were we? Aaah, yeah, last week. Have you ever felt a rainbow that is salted exploding in your mouth? No?  Then you have probably never eaten mutura because that should have been your first reaction to that awesomeness getting in contact with your mandible. Mbugua wa mutura didn’t even wash his hands after handling charcoal. He wiped real quick on his lab coat and progressed to cut me a piece of that roast tube-meat. I learnt that you don’t put salt unto the mutura. No,No. You roll the piece on a patch of salt that is on the chopping board. When that awesomeness landed in my mouth, I had to close my eyes and feel it with every part of my body. It took the intervention of loving comrades to press me to the ground and prevent me from flying off the cosmos with orgasmic impulses that my brain was having, as a function of the contents in my mouth. I turned around and looked at people walking home peacefully and shook my head in sorrow. How can one just  walk past a mutura stall and go to sleep in peace at night? Is that how they plan to spend their valuable time on earth? Yesterday as I was rolling a piece on salt and exercising my jaws for the torture I was going to conduct on the product of my well-spent 20 shilling coin, a lass I had tagged along asked me whether I knew what was in the mutura. I paused for a moment and realized it had been six consecutive days since I started eating mutura and I had no idea what it was yet. It got me into a lot of soul-searching on what uncultured swine I had become . Do you now want to assume that as a sane, Gusii-bred and mannered adult I will continue eating something whose contents I have no knowledge of? Because if you did, you are damn right!

title: A Kisii's Guide to Travelling
creator: Richard

_yoast_wpseo_metadesc: When you are leaving Gusiiland for the capital, there are essentials which you cannot afford to leave without. content:
[nectar_dropcap color="#0a0a0a"]W [/nectar_dropcap]hen you are leaving Gusiiland for the capital, there are essentials which you cannot afford to leave without. If you attempt to sneak out without them, ancestors could send a delegation of witches that would slash your manhood so that you always have to sit when you are taking a piss. Being that I love these your urinals, I could not risk a life where I can’t be able to shower those colorful and nice smelling balls with my excrete juices. I’m talking about the balls in the urinal bawl, not the…never mind. You need to carry your accent like a badge of honor as you travel. Tell the driver, “Gwacha kidogo nikochoe”, when you get to Narok. Even if you do not have the accent, fake it. Terrorize your fellow travelers with questions that are laced with too much accent they are left to ponder whether you are speaking in tongues. Make a phone call when there is silence in the and ask in the characteristic Kisii high pitch, “Ile pag irikuwa na masigwa mrikuumbuka mkanigwekea?” After the call, hold a brief monologue, “mama yiangu anakuwanga na mcheso sana, timanyeti ng’aki akora.” You have to finish off with a little Kisii so that it sounds like a spell. When everyone is dozing off, shout out of the blues, “Gaki nyasaye one okare igoro!” Sit back and watch their horrified faces as they hold on to their seats expecting the bus to float or something and send short prayers to their gods for journey mercies. Your vast knowledge in many a field should be unleashed forth for consultation even when you know nothing more than John Snow. Remember, you are an expert in everything. The wisdom you have acquired should not be left to wash out in vain. If your bus is stopped by traffic officers, rise to the occasion. “Unachua hawa Porisi mimi hawakuwai niwesa. 2004 nilikuwa naendesa canter kutoka Puusia kwenda Nairobi. Wakinisimamisa nawaambia kama wao ni wanaume wanifuate. Hawatakangi kupembeleswa.”

Pretend like you know everything until you believe it yourself; Look how well it worked for Nyakundi.

Even when it is something as mundane as mahindi choma at Suswa, say how these Kalenjins don’t know how to roast it. Inform whoever is sitting next to you how good you are at roasting maize that if they tasted it they would become vegetarians forever. When you get to Sotik, make them wince as you over-zealously disturb the quiet confines of the bus with a fabricated narration on how you once chased Kipsigis warriors through the night towards Kericho when they attempted to steal cattle from your village. Be. The. Sensei! As you were in Gusiiland, always carry with you your knack for unnecessary details. Passing by the University of Nairobi, relate how the senior lecturer in the Veterinary Sciences is a village-mate of yours. Describe with aptness how simple he is although he earns a load of cash. At the Central Bank, say you have a nephew who is a senior manager. Tell your seatmate how you can withdraw money without your ID because you know who is who in there. In fact, go ahead and prove it by making a phone call to this said nephew whom you have saved as, ‘Nephew Central Bank’.

Of course, he will not pick up because that particular person is not even your nephew. The closest thing you share with them is the village borehole.

But because you think everyone else has a yoghurt for a brain, you’ll still beat stories about him. “He is probably buy handling ministers you know, this is where they bank,” Say this to clear any doubts. But you are not a Gusii person if you are not kind. Generosity will ooze from you as you travel as you travel. This is why despite your perennial stupidity and poor sense of fashion (I mean, sport shoes and a full suit really??), the gods of your ancestors will grant you forgiveness. At a lunch stop, you will invite your seatmate if they cannot afford it. If you alight with two sacks of matoke and see some other Kosto moron strut their buttocks away in a sling bag talking about “naenda zangu kula biriani” or some shit like that, you will offer to give them a sack because you don’t understand why they want to fast. Your kindness rises above you like a flag. So, if you happen to see me in a bus ride from Kisii to the city, always remember one thing; I am Kisii before I am your friend. I was born at the foothill of Sameta Hill in the village of Bondonya. I am in the lineage of the great Sakawa, who was a graduate of the Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry. I am a Kisii through and through. If I put up an act, please do not bestow on me the pressure of friendship, I shall switch to it when we get to Nyairobi.

content:
claustrophobia; /ˌklɔːstrəˈfəʊbɪə/ noun extreme or irrational fear of confined places.
Growing up, I used to stay with my maternal grandparents because I feared my mother like hell. She would beat me up as if she was trying to kill a stray snake and that shit hurt like nonsense. I would curse her inwardly and wish for her death. Her parents were however really great folks who would pamper me like the black king I am. I used to ask my grandmother whether she gave birth to my mum while planting pepper because she was bitter as they come. My grandfather, a local high school watchman, used to treat me like a son. He would on several occasions joke to people that I was his lastborn son, Damn I love that man. So I stayed over at my mother's home and even schooled from there till I was past the bed-wetting stage. It is, however, in this very loving environment that I developed my greatest nightmare; Claustrophobia. Across the SDA church that we worshipped at, there lived a guy I used to hang with. Though older than me by about two elections, he considered me his closest friend. The lad had a sister my age, who was as ripe as a banana. Her hot eyes could ripen the rawest of avocados. I used to go over to their house in the pretense of visiting her brother just to have a glimpse of the lass. I indeed confirmed that God gave men two heads, but the heart cannot pump blood to both simultaneously. Blimey! I was thick as a sheep. I would rather not eat the whole day but just hang around their house, just to see her moving her voluptuous self around as I died inside. Anytime she'd talk to me I would just stand there like a stationary piece of flesh and watch her lips move as I nod like the imbecile I was. God, I was thick as hell. Anyway, my grandpa warned me against going to their place after realizing I was spending too much time there and ignoring any other duty I was assigned to around the compound. But my other head would not listen, I would pretend to go check on the cows in the grazing field next to the church, and then crawl across the grass because the area was extremely visible from our compound, hop into the church through one window and out through another into the voluptuous lass's compound. I could feel God bury his face in his palm as I tiptoed across the church benches towards the window, wondering why he wasted precious clay on me. But is it not God who said we should love our neighbors as we love ourselves? I judged I was merely following his commandments and so everything was justified. On this fateful day, I did a really silly thing. God, I was stupid. Every time I recall this particular bit I feel like the greatest embarrassment to the African race and its clients. When I got to the voluptuous lass's compound, I pretended as always to ask for her brother. She duly informed me he was not in and I could stick around and see whether he would come back soon. The lass herself was about to take a shower and said I could sit and wait for her to get done so we could talk as we waited for the brother. Her mentioning going to the shower was a great mistake because then, blood started to flow to the wrong head of the two that are gifted to men. Seeing this, she giggled and told me not to feel shy as she knew I had a crush on her and that she would fix me up once she was out of the shower - if I knew what she meant.

I didn't know fam. I did not have a clue what she meant by fixing me up, and that is when I made a very terrible assumption.

I stripped. Yes I did. When she went to shower, I thought she would come back for some action. So I stripped right there in their living room and only left my "cowboy" innerwear and sat waiting for her. When she came back and opened the door, guess who was standing right there next to her?
Ooh God, God, God!
Her brother was standing there beside her, staring back at me as I did to both of them. The mental shock was tremendous. Sitting right there naked on the seat, I can't tell you what I was feeling. 'Cause I can't tell you. I just can't tell you. I can't tell you anything. I was half-dead. I was so dead that when the brother asked me what was wrong, I just lost it. "Ants! Ants! Ants!" I shouted as I started wriggling on the seat. They went out and brought some herbal leaf which they rubbed on me as I continued screaming. My clothes had to be thoroughly scrutinized to ensure no "ants" had stayed behind to torture me later. They swept the whole place to ensure "ants" were all gone. Ants I had created to salvage my situation. Let me never hear any of you losers telling this story somewhere else because it took a lot of courage to type. As I walked home at sundown happy to have escaped so close from the two siblings, I found that the cows were untied from the grazing field. That only meant that my grandpa had not gone for watchman duties that particular day and so my turkey was roast. I resolved that if he as much tried to quarrel me about my whereabouts, I'd tell him to go kiss a porcupine if so he wished. He didn't. As in, he did not ask me where I was, and that was as strange as it was pleasant. After dinner, the old man asked me to his bedroom.

"There are some lemons that rolled down under my bed I need you to get for me, " he said to me. Peeking down, I could not see any lemons. " There is nothing here, " I told him, my head still under the bed. " Move further in," he beckoned.

It was only when I heard the door getting locked behind me that I knew I had led myself to my own trap. The old man pulled out a whip from his cupboard drawer and started to work on my ass - the only thing popping from where I was kneeling under the bed. My mind went berserk as he whooped me, shouting about how he had many times warned me from staying in people's houses all day. I was trapped, not able to move to the front as my head hit the bedroom wall, or to the back where my ass was about to ignite from the whipping. I only needed to fart, and the whole place would blow up because of the charged air. In that small space, I screamed and panicked and felt life slip out of me. I struggled like a caged rat as oxygen failed me. I begged for mercy with the last words that could be formed by my failing human form. I almost wrote my will from down there with my fingers had I not lost energy. When he finally let me free, I had to rush out to take deep breaths. The bedroom was small, and that did not help with the ventilation problem. I developed my fear of closed spaces. I would to this day rather walk from Kisii to Nairobi than be in a bus that has all its windows closed because I would think I'm going to die. If there are stairs in a building, I will take them rather than ride on those devil's instruments you people call elevators. They say the best way to deal with your fears is by facing them. That would be easy for me if that fear was not death. Miss me with that.

content: Samburu is a large, beautiful wallpaper. The kind that you want people to know you use. The type that you continuously unlock your phone screen for in a matatu so other miserable passengers with Ronaldo wallpapers can see. The acacia trees making canopies on the landscape are dotted with sleeping hills in the backdrop which makes you regret why you have never thought of visiting before. The hills themselves have rough rocks sticking out like a chin that is not well shaven or a bosom of a beautiful body that is the countryside. As I set my foot in this part of the world and looked at the magnificent beauty spread ahead of me, I whispered ashe oleng'  to the Samburu god and prepared to write a letter to my men, Rao. I have made it to Canaan; I don't think I'll be coming back. [caption id="attachment_6076" align="aligncenter" width="275"] One of Samburu's awesome hills. Look at that neck goddamit![/caption]   The only time my village ass was ever out of Bondonya, the village at the bottom of Sameta Hill in the banana republic of Kisii was when I would get sick, and the herbs that my mother planted outside our house did not work. Coming to this your capital city to undertake my high school studies was a huge move for me. It meant I had to use another language that is not Gusii for the next phase of my life. My uncultured tongue almost committed suicide while making conversation with the sophisticated kids of Nairobi. I almost died of choking in form two when I tried to pronounce the word "kebab." A moment which forced my saliva to go the wrong pipe and luckily got saved by a desk mate who hit my back so hard as if he was spanking a bull. It is in this school that I came to see a wild animal for the first time live live¸ as my folks would say. These were monkeys that roamed the school because of its bush-like setting. The next time I ever saw a wild animal was in Kenyatta University at around 10 pm of every day, when a flock of hyenas congregates outside the Nyayo hostels to shoot their shot at first-year damsels. You can, therefore, imagine the excitement I had when I was in a tour vehicle on my way to Samburu Intrepids, a tented luxury camp inside the Samburu National Reserve. It is set on raised decks which overlook the Ewaso Nyiro River with its rich endowment of wildlife. Coming from the gate to Intrepids is an approximately 30Km ride and if you're lucky you'll be able to the see the Samburu special five animals, which are a breed that you cannot see anywhere else in this land. I had to jot down the names because they were so strange; the Reticulated giraffe, Grevy's zebra, gerenuk, Somali ostrich and Beisa oryx. The most peculiar name I had ever heard up until that point was Kebab, which to date sounds like a name you'd give a village dog. On our way to the camp, we were lucky enough to meet a Grevy's zebra, which is basically a donkey that grew up in Runda instead of carrying water in the village. A donkey with expensive makeup “The grevy's Zebra is special because of the uniqueness of its stripes," said the tour guide. As if in sync, we pulled out phones to take snaps of it. It passed right in front of the tour vehicle swinging its buttocks as if aware that we saw it. Reminded me how I lose my natural walk when passing in front of a large group of females, it did. "Do you have a kebab's Zebra?" I asked the tour guide ‘Huh?" "Naah, nothing," I replied as I almost died with internal laughter. When we got to the Intrepids camp, we were directed towards the Heritage Hotels bar area that is just along the shores of Ewaso Nyiro. The manager joked that there might be occasional interruptions by snakes from below. I have never heard of a worse joke in my life. You don't joke about snakes, the most terrible creatures of nature. I fear those things like nonsense. I'd preferably be locked in a den of lions like Daniel, and maybe God might send his angels to save me. You've never heard anywhere, not even in Greek mythology, of a person who got along with snakes. Heck, if I were a snake, I wouldn't even go near a snake. I'd practice celibacy because I can't copulate with a fellow hideous creature. Anyway, I had to shift places closer to the bar's counter to feel safer. We were asked to order any drink from the bar, and my damsel and l did justice to that menu. I will not say which drinks they were, but they were strong enough to wade off the fear of snakes. Ooh, by the way, any expense we were incurring was catered for courtesy of Samburu Intrepids. I will not disclose the amount, but for your curiosity, if I showed up in my village with the money worth our expenses, I would start a wizardry school that would rival egoistical Hogwarts (They keep rejecting my applications!). There were a few Jorocheres at the hotel, who I noticed were referred to as wageni as compared to us locals, but I am not complaining. After a few drinks, dinner was ready. I almost laughed at the thought; I was going to eat dinner. Haha. Look at me now haters; I am eating dinner. Dinner. Haha. Such a strange word. From the days of my mother shouting shauri yenu!, to eating ugali omena pale Villa Rosa Kibandaski at campus, now here I was about to devour dinner in a five-star hotel in the middle of a National Reserve next to colonizers. Haha. Someone slap the fuck out of me because I need to wake up from this dream. Anyway, dinner. Dinner is not the typical meal you idiots are used to at your bedsitters, where you wash your hands at the kitchen sink and proceed to demolish your mountain of ugali with sukuma mayai, sited on a 20 liter jerrican of water. No. Here you eat like a colonizer. A man of nyadhi. There is a napkin that you place on your lap to prevent the food from making you dirty or something. I found that hilarious because back at my crib, my caretaker had just cut water supply to my place since I had delayed paying rent. I had developed a long-distance relationship with the shower, and therefore I was the one going to make the food dirty here. But cha muhimu ni uhai. The table was laid out well with cutlery for everything. Sijui different spoons for soup, another one for dessert, another for the main course.  Strange, because back at the crib I am in possession of one big spoon that doubles as a mwiko and also cooks vegetables. It is also the same spoon that I eat with. Lakini as we said, cha muhimu ni uhai.  There also were forks, knives and other strange thingamajigs I began to wonder whether they had cooked an elephant for us. The scenes on that dinner table were straight out of an anatomy dissections class. I had lived to see the day my primary school insha topic came to life; Siku ya kukata na shoka, or was it ndovu kumla mwananawe. Gosh! I don't know man; my village ass might have been high. Those drinks were pretty strong. Because I have never dined like this before, I decided to follow what my damsel was doing because she grew up in the city and might know those shenanigans. I followed her cue, and I copied as perfect as I do most of my school lab reports. I did not want to embarrass myself before these fine hosts. I reckon I might have introduced myself as her when the hosts enquired. Heck, it was the drinks. Must have been. Damn free drinks. As we ate and listened to hyenas laughing from the shores of Nyiro River and leopards purr from top of trees around us, we talked of the next day's plans which were mostly about a Safari drive. The waiters kept asking whether we were comfortable. The fuck, are we comfortable? Get out man, this is the most comfortable a Kisii ass can get. I was shown to my room, and the sight of it had me wondering why there are only five stars to rate it. Crap man, this stuff was the whole damn solar system, whatchu talkin' ‘bout five stars. The bathroom had soap and shampoos of different types and for different body parts. This was also hilarious considering as a practicing bachelor I use the same soap for my dishes, clothes, and body. Yeeea, sneer away you idiots. You don't have to eat at my place now if you visit me. Writing is exhausting, and you people need to start paying.  I will continue with the next part of the story soon. Lemme go eat a kebab.

content:
If you’ve never woken up to the chitter of exotic birds, then you need to start paying for me taking my time to explain to you the kind of paradise I woke up to in the Samburu National Reserve. You wanna know what is a better feeling than the sleep after a thorough beating? Waking up to the chink-chink-chink-chink-chink calls from the Acryllium vulturinum, a species of the guinea fowl. I have to admit though, the bird has an ugly head but a beautiful body and voice.  
Reminded me of one of the ladies in Nairobi diaries.
It’s closest relative I was informed, is the white breasted Guinea fowl, and please do not start getting any stupid images from its name. You went to read that again, didn’t ya? And you wonder why this country cannot progress. So I wake up in the morning, use different bar soaps for every body part, brush my teeth real sharp like I am going to eat at an NYS hotel and then dress up for the animal kingdom because in about an hour, we head out for a game drive! I step out of my room and walk down to the dining area for breakfast and it’s as if all the corruption money was dumped in this place. There is a chef preparing eggs live live. Sijui omelette, sunny side up, hard scrambled eggs, eggs with cheese and whatnot. There were other mini-chefs assisting folks to pack unfamiliar dishes at the buffet table. As a child, breakfast at the foot of Sameta Hill in the village of Bondonya was white porridge with distant rumors of sugar.
If you were lucky enough, you would be the one to lick the Sufuria used to prepare porridge at the end, because it was sugarlier at the bottom.
So I took a plate and approached the buffet table which was at that time full of Jorochere. I sent a short prayer for protection from my ancestors, because the last time and African man dined with these folks he found himself on a boat in the Atlantic Ocean, never to return home. I decided if bad, bad and proceeded to serve myself until I was sure everyone in the line behind me was uneasy. A warm towel was already set for me to wipe my hands with, and I have never felt more like royalty before. I felt like a Zamunda King on a trip to Wakanda, but of course you folks wouldn’t understand this because the last time you were tended to nicely, you were being circumcised After heavy swallowing, I escorted my stomach to the safari vehicle. By this time, the sun was about 20 minutes away from rising and we all got psyched to record it emerge from the beautiful Samburu hills in the backdrop of the reserve. As a human who absolutely abhors waking up early, I have never seen the sun come out of its nest. The closest I come is when one of you cultured high end people posts photos with a filtered Nairobi sunrise pretending to be the hardest working people in the pothole capital of Kenya. The best thing happened that morning; as we were driving to get a sweet spot to record the rising African sun, we stumbled upon a lone lion devouring breakfast. Has your village self ever witnessed a sunrise next to a lion having its morning meal? Is it not you who was bragging that you are sun kissed on a WhatsApp status while next to dogs celebrating their mating season in your village? So yeah. Basically I experienced the most beautiful moments of my life. Afterwards, we drove around the reserve while greeting every other animal we could find while taking pictures, because how would you believe my tale anyway? Our time of visit coincided with the dry season in this locale. Hence, the Ewaso Nyiro River which flows from Mt. Kenya was uncharacteristically dry.
The animals were struggling to quench their thirst, a reminder that dry spells were not exclusively affecting campus comrades.
The elephants though being very crafty, dig the river bed with their enormous feet and suck the earth of its hidden waters with their trunk. We observed their genius work but were advised to not make noises while at it. Basically, not to address the elephant in the room. The tour guide educated us about the conflict that the local Samburu community has been having with wild animals. Occasionally, elephants and lions walk out and invade their homes but mutual efforts have ensured a peaceful co-existence. The best part? There are very rare cases of Samburu people involving themselves in poaching activities. Beautiful people those ones with beautiful faces and hearts. We finished the game drive after three hours of thorough sight-seeing, before me and my lady felt like we should have jumped off the Safari vehicle and looked for George of the jungle to help us stay longer. Also, I don’t want to write more about my great visit there because I’m a struggling writer who has been playing hide and seek with the landlord; I do not desire for you to enjoy my little joys of life with me. Gang, until my next travel, I thought I should let you know that Samburu was a bomb!  

“I always wonder why birds choose to stay in the same place when they can fly anywhere on the earth, then I ask myself the same question.”

content:
So this is how it ends.

With the muzzle of this gun kissing my temples. The taste of my salty tears at the edges of my lips, these lips that I used to kiss you with. These lips that won't live to kiss another day. The tears are clouding my vision but ironically maintaining my unfathomable clarity of your figures on this bed. Limp, naked and cold. Did it have to end this way? On our matrimonial bed? A gift at our wedding. I remember with unflinching clarity our smiles with palms clasped in each other. We couldn't wait. The sermon was too long for us, the reception mundane, the sun deliberately slow to set. We wanted to get lost in the passion of our bodies together, forever. Tearing each other's tops on this bed that night. This bed that you now lie cold, motionless and in the arms of another. This bed that I now sit, with my index finger on the trigger. With the muzzle kissing my temples. I can hear voices on the ground floor. Do you hear them my love?
Oh sorry - you can't. I forgot.
You are lost deep in the arms of another. I can now hear their footsteps, they must have heard the shots. These machines should all be made silent you know. But they won't find me because I would have joined you. Dead, but not naked like you. And definitely not in the arms of another, like you are. I hope they will understand and forgive me. But is there really forgiveness for people like you and me? And your secret lover? Does even the devil forgive two adulterers and one murderer? The same devil that I know they will blame in tomorrow's Daily. Did it have to end like this, ineluctably caught in savage circumstances. So this is how it ends. On our matrimonial bed. With sheets soaked red, reeking of not your hymen but of your betrayal. I have consigned to oblivion. They are almost at the door. I cannot stay behind. Why would I stay behind with this drudgery of life? A life excruciatingly disappointing. So this is how it ends. With my index finger on the trigger. Wrenching events and a sentence to the world. I knew love, but love did not know me.

title: Book Review — We wish to inform you that tomorrow we will be killed with our families.
creator: Solomonjesse

_yoast_wpseo_metadesc: Review of "We wish to inform you that tommorow we will be killed with our Families", a book on the horrors of the Rwanda genocide. Review by Jesse Solomon.

content: I have read numerous books and watched a number of movies which sought to narrate the genocide that took place in Rwanda in 1994 that left slightly more than a million Tutsis dead. Left To Tell, a personal account by Emily Illibagaeza left me close to tears. By the time we finished the movie Hotel Rwanda by Paul Rusesabagina, the entire hall was jerking tears. However, reading We wish to inform you that tomorrow we shall be killed with our children was entirely a new and fresh experience, a kind of vuja de. In this book, the author takes a multi-dimensional look at the entire Rwandan genocide. He narrates the history of the breeding hate between the two polarized groups, the Hutus and Tutsis, tracing it back to the Hamitic theory that was coined by John Speke, a theory that claimed that the long-nosed Tutsis and Ethiopians are the original ancestors of Africa thus placing them at the top of the social tier. The theory is perpetuated by the Belgian colonists when they put the Tutsis in power during the colonial period, aggravating the Hutus even more. When the Hutus rise to power in their fight for independence, they continue in their hatred and seek to exterminate the Tutsis completely out of the sheer belief that the Tutsis, as long as they are alive, would pose a threat to them. The author goes further and analyzes the masterminds behind the massacre. He argues that the basic organization of the Hutus into interhamwe, was just a surface level organization to show that the massacre had been thoroughly planned and that though the international community had received warnings prior to the massacre that saw so many people killed within a span of three months, they did nothing to quell the lasting hatred. We are also shown the reaction of the African community at that time, how the states within the region took polarized positions with regard to the situation. Congo, under Mobutu Sese Seko, took full support for the Hutu killings and provided a perfect avenue where the French could supply arsenal to the Hutus; arsenal that was later shipped into Rwanda to facilitate the killing. Countries like Uganda on the other hand, though did not intervene in the massacre, join hands and fight alongside the FAR, the army that liberated Rwanda from the Hutu oppression and together out Mobutu, bringing Kabila into power. The book has essentially interwoven the various factors that came into play before, during and after the massacre. This is a literary piece that is a combination of history and emotion. Reading this book left a number of questions in my mind. The silence of the international community was more of a call for Africa, as a continent to stand up and defend itself. We have, for long been dependent on the international intervention, which is not always best, as evidenced by how the international community handled the prosecution of the perpetrators of the massacre. Even after the genocide, Rwanda was left on the brink of teetering in abject poverty, but all through, had risen from the ashes of the war, which is something I believe the whole of Africa should look up to. If you are a lover of history, whether or not you have had enough about the Rwandan massacre, I believe this book is worth your read. This is a volume of pages that will take you through an emotional rollercoaster, making you question the where the humane humanity is, but close with restoring your faith in humanity, as you look at how the Tutsis though struggling with pain, strove to forgive the Hutus and work hand in hand with them towards building a better country.

content:
The campus bachelor is an animal. A wild untamed animal whose ways of survival in the campus jungle cannot be contained even in several volumes of a NatGeo special. He hunts through the day and during the night; leaving you bemused and wondering whether he really ever has time to rest. The campus bachelor is unlike any other, he has no childish tendencies like a high-school teenager nor the disillusioned illusions of adulting that accompany the graduate bachelor.
Ah-ah. No.
He knows how to dodge the bullets that life shoots his way. Life gives him lemons, he eats the lemons and spits back the seeds in life’s bitchy face.  
For the campus bachelor is his own man. He answers to no one.
  Well, he answers to no one except his parents. And his landlord maybe. Apart from that, he answers to no one. Okay, maybe a few his older siblings if he has one. And his caretaker sometimes. Otherwise, it is crystal clear that he answers to no one at all. His phone is a normal Infinix or Tecno, large enough to keep him off the embarrassment of having a kabambe and cheap enough to feel embarrassed when he meets a ninja with an iPhone. He doesn’t really care about the phone type. Except when in a group of pretty damsels who are rocking high-end devices and tablets. Otherwise, he doesn’t really care. Well, maybe a bit if he needs to take a selfie with a lass he just met and the camera of his Infishit, sorry, Infinix, is 2MP. Then he has to care. But otherwise, he doesn’t, as long as ninacall na ninachat sijali msee. Yes, hajali msee. But he will always ask you how much you bought your phone for.
It’s not like he cares how much your S6 cost you, he just wants to estimate how many days of ugali mayai all that 50 Gs would have pushed him through.
Mimi nikibuy simu ya more than 10K lazima ikuje na nudes. Otherwise, as long as he accesses his WhatsApp he is fine with his smaller device. He used to have Whatsapp GB because he particularly liked the dark theme that came with it, but he had to delete it.  

His crush had put up a meme about guys who use GB WhatsApp wearing Samosa underwear. You know, those ones with the name Cowboy or Manchester United running across its top band.

  So, he laughed and laughed and deleted his GB. Otherwise, he doesn’t really care how his phone looks. Unless he has 15MB of data, then he will restrict the background access to use that data for a couple of hours for checking his fantasy football Whatsapp group. And sneak for some twitter banter about Nyakundi having his account suspended twice. When he takes a screenshot, he will rotate the phone and have it in landscape so that his screenshot looks like it’s from a tablet. Otherwise, he is fine. Ooh, he might also disable the background restriction, open his Spotify app so that his notification bar looks lit in the screenshot he sends you. Otherwise, he never cares about your opinion. [caption id="attachment_6436" align="aligncenter" width="300"] Of course this is not in reference to our said Campus Bachelor[/caption] The campus bachelor doesn’t care about your opinion unless of course, it hurts him. If he writes a blog post he will say he doesn’t really care about how many people read it, but how much impact it had on the few who read it. But he will constantly check the comments on facebook to see what you are saying. He will send the link to a few friends and he will not wait for their feedback. Although he will just ask what you thought about it, it’s not like he cares about your opinion. Someone else already told him he is the greatest writer since King Solomon wrote his mixtape in the bible (those songs were many!). Yeah, so he knows he is awesome. But he would really love to hear your opinion, and hers, and his, and yours and the other one’s. But it’s not like he cares about them. But he would love to hear them. If he has a joke he thinks is very funny, he will post it on twitter or maybe facebook or his WhatsApp status. If it takes 20 minutes before he gets retweets or a response, he will pull it down for reasons that are not him feeling bad that you did not find it funny. The campus bachelor eats where he wants because he is not here to impress you. He will eat in Klabu and gobble down his ugali matumbo. Or in Blessed Hotel somewhere in the pockets of K.U. where he will down his ugali-omena and wash it down with tap water. He doesn’t care whether you are seeing him. Well, unless maybe you are his crush then he will eat chapo beef somewhere else with a fork and spoon. Though he really doesn’t mind who sees him eating mutura na supu right along the road. But he might not eat if he sees a pretty youngin he recognizes from Instagram. Not that she knows him or is afraid of what she thinks,

But you don’t drink chang’aa in a den opposite Kenya Breweries.

It’s not like that quote makes sense, so don’t overthink it. The campus bachelor does not have a girlfriend because he thinks love is a bullet to the brain of those who hallucinate the idea that one human being was meant to satisfy the emotional requirements of another. But oh, how he wishes that bullet hits him sometimes. But he says he is his own man, not answerable of his ways to any girl. But of course, it would be awesome if someone’s daughter warmed his bed sometime. He has learned the art of patience when he does his hunting. After all, the dry spell he carries has him making calculated moves like a thirsty pregnant gecko in the Sahara Desert. He will be chatting up 20 mamis in his DM for 2 weeks so that is he fails, at least 2 of them will have fallen his way. He walks with a packet of sure condoms he picked up in the school hostel washrooms. What he has no clue is that they expired about a month gone. How would he know really? Education is what brought the campus bachelor here. He studies sometimes, sometimes he relies on formation. His GPA is lower than his dressing skills. But he doesn’t care much. Or so he says. He believes innovation is everything, creativity is greater than education that is dictated to you by professors who are too lazy to explain what they are reading from the handout. He doesn’t have any innovative idea yet. But he will in good time. He talks about Zuckerberg, Bill Gates and Richard Branson being geniuses of their industries without college degrees although he has never come up with anything creative past copy pasting twitter jokes to his WhatsApp status. Exams might make him study and panic and run around looking for notes but that should not make you think he cares about education. Thoughts of dropping out and being rich have definitely crossed his mind, but then he will have to survive on his own. Not like he survives on other people, maybe his parent’s money a bit. But then he is an independent man. He tried to bet sometime back and he lost a lot. But he swears to Dedan Kimathi’s grave, Siku moja hawa sportpesa nitawakuta. He swears and curses like a ma’fucker. Huyo Mhindi wallahi shit atanitambua. We can only wait for his fantasies to come true. For the night is dark and full of terror and the campus bachelor is broke and hopes for better.

title: Book Review — The Kite Runner
creator: Solomonjesse

_yoast_wpseo_metadesc: A detailed book Review of Kite Runner by Khaled Hosseini. in the Voice of an African child done by blogger/writer Jesse Solomon.

comments.author: Jante Joy Juma
comments.content: This is one of my favourite books! Loved to read a new perspective on it :-)

comments.author: Jante Joy Juma
comments.content: This is one of my favourite books! Loved to read a new perspective on it :-)

content: The Kite Runner- Khaled Hosseini. I had to read this book twice, and yet each time was as refreshing as the first time. One thing I really loved about this book, and about Hosseini’s writing in general (His two other books) is how he plays around with simple English words to come up with really creative sentences, almost like Paul Kalanithi. Reading this book, I felt that every word mattered, that if one sentence was removed from this 340 paged book, the story wouldn’t have made sense. Hosseini’s writing is ridden with pure emotion. The relationship between the characters is pristine, even if flawed, which makes the writing even more riveting. The Kite Runner is a fictional narration that stretches over a span of more than twenty-five years. The overarching themes presented in this literary text include friendship, guilt, war and tribal oppression. Hosseini begins the book by painting the strong bond of friendship between Amir, a Pashtun, and Hassan, a Hazara. In Afghanistan, the Hazaras are treated as slaves to the Pashtuns for the longest time in history. By creating a sense of camaraderie between a Pashtun and a Hazara, Hosseini presents the main conflict in the story- the indecisiveness of a Pashtun boy on whether to bask in the glory of his tribe or stand by his friendship with a Hazara. It would be easy for Amir to recoil and enjoy the privileges of being Pashtun were it not for the circumstantial factors that led to the friendship between him and Hassan. Amir’s father, Baba, shares a strong friendship with Hassan’s father, Ali, despite them being from tribes that have master-slave relations, having grown up together. They treat each other as brothers. Amir and Hassan grow up together too, and even feed off the same breast- that of a Hazara woman- as Amir’s mother dies during his birth and Hassan’s mother elope immediately after he’s born. The two children spend quality time together, with Amir reading Hassan stories under their favorite pomegranate tree as Hassan is illiterate. In their friendship, more than once does Hassan stand up for Amir. When Assef, a rich Pashtun wants to hit Amir with brass knuckles, it is Hassan who steps in, pointing his slingshot at Assef, and warning his that he would destroy his eye. During the kite-running, Kabul’s favorite children’s game, Hassan helps Amir fly his kite and eventually, win. It is Hassan who runs after the last fallen kite, and even when cornered by Assef and his friends, he refuses to hand the kite over. He lets Assef sodomize him, as long as he delivers the kite to Amir (a very grotesque narration). Amir witnesses Hassan being raped, but out of fear, runs away crying. Later, Hassan comes home bleeding, and hands the kite over to Amir, who chooses to ignore the guilt simmering inside him. He takes the kite to his father, hoping that would win the affection and attention he has been fighting for ever since he was born. Since then, even though Amir receives the affection from his father (which is short-lived), he is forced to live with the guilt of not standing up for Amir. War breaks out in Afghanistan, and both Amir and his Baba leave the country for America under very strenuous circumstances. In America, Amir studies, becomes a writer and marries. Later, after his father dies, he receives a call from Rahim Khan, his childhood friend, asking him to go to Afghanistan. ‘There is a way to be good again,’ Rahim Khan says. Amir flies back to be told that Hassan died and left behind a son, Sohrab. Rahim also informs him that Hassan wasn’t Ali’s son, but Baba’s thus, his half-brother. Hosseini later narrates of the struggles Amir faces as he tries to rescue Sohrab. Reading this book, I was left wondering why people do good deeds. Baba builds orphanages, helps the poor, and even risks his life in some instances to save other people supposedly out of guilt. It is the guilt that he brought forth an illegitimate child, Hassan, born outside the wedlock that compels him to be kind. Baba and Amir, his son, are in many ways similar as guilt drives the father as much as the son. Amir risks death too, in trying to save Sohrab, arguably out of guilt that he didn’t stand for Hassan, his half-brother while he was raped. Is it possible that guilt can be channeled properly to make the best of us as human beings? Hosseini paints Baba as a non-religious person, simply driven by guilt to do good to other people. What are the chances, therefore, of being moral, without being religious?

title: Book Review — Sula
creator: Solomonjesse

_yoast_wpseo_metadesc: Sula is a book by Toni Morrison and is reviewed by Jesse Solomon on Voice of an African Child comprehensively. Pick on what he learnt and what you might.
_yoast_wpseo_bctitle: Book Review of Toni Morrison's Sula by Jesse Solomon on Voice of an African child.

content:
Sula is amongst the numerous Toni Morrison’s publications, set in Medallion, a black community in Ohio. The book’s timing stretches over half of the 20th century, starting from 1918 and ending in 1965. In this book, Morrison addresses the social dynamics of the blacks in America with a specific focus on two female friends, Sula and Nel. Most of the events unfolding in this novel, arranged chronologically according to the years they happen, revolve around these two characters, and seek to highlight how relationships can withstand the test of time whilst its members interact with the community at large, concurrently. The Bottom, which is the black’s locale, is set parallel to the Medallion valley, where the whites live, to bring to light the relationship between these two different races at the time. Whilst the whites rarely visit Bottom, the blacks often go to the valley in search of employment opportunities such as working at The Bridge, which they are mostly denied. Any intimate relationship between the blacks and the whites, despite the two being close neighbors, is regarded a taboo, which emphasizes the racial segregation in the then community. For example, Sula is considered an outcast in The Bottom because she is rumored to have had sexual relations with the white men.

In this book, Toni Morrison explores the idea of family, friendship and women empowerment. A striking example portrayed by the author, once again, is the friendship between Sula and Nel. It can be argued that at the time these two meet, both of them are disempowered by their immediate family members. Sula, for example, is perpetually neglected by her mother who focuses more on having frequent but casual sex with men around The Bottom. Given that Sula’s father had already died, she grows up with minimum parental tutelage. Nel, on the other hand, is born to an absentee father, Mr. Wright, who always works as a bargeman and rarely comes back home to spend time with his family. Her mother, having a strong Christian background, forcefully cultures her into being someone else, a figure that would be accepted and respected in the society rather than remaining true to herself. Her mother constantly tells her to not sit idly but instead, “…pull your nose.” (Sula 28) Nel’s mother’s continued efforts in getting her to pull her nose despite the fact that Nel doesn’t like it signifies how while growing up, she is forced to adopt a personality that she does not necessarily like.

The bond of friendship between Sula and Nel grows stronger with each passing day. For example, Nel’s quits her dream of leaving Medallion in order to discover herself, and redefine her ‘me-ness,’ when she meets Sula. Morrison narrates how the two grow so close that ‘…a compliment to one was a compliment to another, and a cruelty to one, a challenge to another” (Sula 84). Many are the instances when the two stand for each other during the tempting times. For example, to fight the boys who taunt them every day while they are walking home from school, Sula cuts her finger, then dares the boys to imagine what she would do to them if she had the courage to do that to herself, which sends them off in fear. Morrison, in describing the Sula-Nel friendship, states that “Their meeting was fortunate, for it let them use each other to grow on” (Sula 52). Through this friendship, both Sula and Nel are able to rise above their familial constraints and discover a newer and more authentic version of themselves.

Morrison is keen to stress the importance of maintaining one’s individuality by showing the danger of losing oneself in any given relationship: be it friendship, courtship, or even marriage. This is seen when Sula seduces Jude, Nel’s husband, and has sex with him even though Sula and Nel had been close friends. Nel, having ceded her individuality and free-spirited nature to Jude in marriage, feels an aching longing when he departs and leaves her with the children.  Nel thinks, “And what am I supposed to do with these thighs now, just walk up and down these rooms? (Sula 111). This reveals her loneliness and the desperate desire to have a man, if not Jude, in her life. Sula, on the other hand, remains adamantly indifferent towards Nel’s pain of betrayal, explaining that growing up, they both viewed sex as a casual pastime, and that therefore, Nel should not harbor negative emotions in her because of the incident. In their conversation, Sula asks Nel, “About who was good. How do you know it was you?” (Sula 136)

Whether Sula’s actions and attitudes are morally acceptable or even justifiable is left for the reader to decide. However, Morrison portrays Sula as the ultimate embodiment of an empowered woman who defies the social conventions and refuses to conform to the banal and subservient position of a woman in Medallion and the black society as a whole. Later, in the book, Sula explains that the rest of the women are “Dying. Just like me. But the difference is, they dying like a stump. Me, I’m going down like one of those redwoods. I sure did live in this world” (Sula 143). By so doing, Sula brings to light the everlasting suppression that women in her society are quietly experiencing. In this conversation with Nel, Sula is able to show how by taking a different path from the rest of the Medallion women she has led a defiant, enjoyable, and in her perspective, well-lived life. Through Sula, Morrison implies that even though women who stand against oppression and seek self-liberation might be hated, the society will later come to love them for choosing to be their most authentic selves.
