content:
I had initially planned to name this post 20 lessons at twenty years; that was way back when I was 19 and still eating food with a spoon direct from the sufuria like the savage I was. Now that I eat directly with my hands, I feel a lot wiser not to structure this post as 20 lessons since I am not entirely sure I can raise exactly 20 of them; could be more or less. Anyway, on September 9th twenty years ago my very beautiful mother gave birth to me as her first born, or so she told everyone. We haven’t found out if this is a lie yet. I feel like I owe the world a peek into a bit of the knowledge I have acquired so far (Well, I owe the world that and China a few coins but that’s beside the point). Or we could start with lessons one being that I need to learn Mandarin at this rate, so I could have an upper hand in case things go South. Or is it East? Well, I don’t know about cardinal points and coordinates since I decided not to study Geography. The rest is History. Haha, I’m not sure you saw what I did there but let’s move on swiftly. Nobody owes you anything in this world. Scratch that, nobody owes you shit! The faster you learn that, the better your chances of having a level head at progressing in life. Not your friends, not someone who told you they will hook you up with an opportunity, not your boss. No single person owes you shit. No one ever loses their sleep at night because of your problems, except yourself. So do not go around placing high expectations in people because no one wakes up and does anything because they are thinking of you. Get your stuff together and hustle your way around things. This does not mean people are not kind. There is a lot of good folk out there who will go out of their way to help you when crap hits the fan but it isn’t something that they owe you. That said, you don’t owe anyone shit.
A friend of mine told me I cannot be a healer when I am an empty well.
Do not carry the burdens of other people unnecessarily. Be kind and generous but do not tolerate nonsense that doesn’t concern you. Never compare yourself with someone who is in a worse situation if you want to make an upward trajectory. Humans have been conditioned to believe that you are in a better life if someone is having it worse. That if you are eating just one meal a day you should be grateful because someone hasn’t eaten in a week. That if you have one bad parent you should be grateful because someone else is an orphan. That if you are earning 20K a month you should be thankful because someone else is earning barely 100 shillings a day. Well, I call utter BS on that. If you want to see yourself make zero development then shine your way in mediocre comparisons. That doesn’t also mean that you heap unnecessary pressure in yourself to make things work for you real quick. Hard work is much underrated. Coupled with patience, working on whatever you have however small can take you to heights you cannot imagine. I also cannot imagine these heights and that’s why you need to trust me. Religion is a very complicated issue that has no outright correct position. Most of us are in a certain belief system because that’s where we were born in. You cannot, therefore, judge other people because you think your religion is greater.
Just hold on to what you feel is right and walk your way.
You could have been worshiping a goat’s dropping for all we care and it would take a little brain-washing through a small holy manual that describes the omnipotence of goat droppings and you will be allover insulting other religions that worship cow droppings and calling it bull shit. Well, it literally is, but you get my point. Education is the greatest thing that could happen to you. I’m not saying literacy or knowing how to say a few things or those lectures in Kenyan campuses where you just copy handed down notes through your life. I’m talking reading books far and wide, reading a Harry Potter Book, a Swahili novel, a sheng blog post, a great newspaper article, a New York Times opinion column, a book on space and time, Isaac Newton’s Principle of Natural Mathematics, the beginning of Buddhism, the Colonial History of Angola. I’m saying reading stuff that doesn’t concern whatever your area of career pursuit is. There are a lot of gems to find in the writings of humans.

Sex is important but supporting your friend’s craft is more important. Watch their YouTube videos, read their blogs, buy their art, listen to their music. It won’t cost you much.

Cabbage is trash because it’s basically just like eating grass that has been fried with tomatoes; leave it to the cows you idiot. Chapati and avocado are overrated, not to forget that thing you call pizza which is basically another chapati with tomato toppings. Kenyatta University guards are full of crap, and they really need to know that we are not at an NYS camp. Liquor in the form of spirits is poison and beer is just herbs soup. Do not say yes to Windows OS updates; they will either crash your pc or freeze some other functionalities. Do not use your phone next to a window in a matatu when in Nairobi CBD even if you think your window is made of wood. Never miss the exit at Roysambu because you will have to start a new life at Githurai. There is more theft in most parts of Nairobi way more than in Githurai. I’m saying, you are more likely to lose your stuff in South B as compared to Githurai. The future is written in Code, literal code, and so find a way of getting involved in the technology space as soon as you can. Whether we like it or not, Reggae will always be the greatest genre of music and that’s why we say it can never be stopped.

You never hear anyone say nobody can stop Mugithi because we can literally walk into those bars in Kiambu and chase everyone out like Jesus did at the temple.

A man who can cook is sexier to everyone that one who can take a female out; go argue with your ancestors. Twitter is the greatest social media network because of the information, Reddit is second because of the memes and YouTube because of the vines. WhatsApp is however pointless and so is constant texting. Messi is a god and Sia is a goddess. Eminem is the greatest. You don’t have to keep tabs with toxic family members no matter who they are; you owe them nothing. Traveling to new places is important, it is the best thing you can ever do with your money. Loving someone’s daughter is a great emotion, knowing someone’s daughter loves you with all her heart is the greatest human emotion.
Life is actually not short, it is the longest experience you will ever have.
Finally a gem from my dear mother; don’t worry when others think they are doing better than you, you are your greatest competitor.

content:
I walked home with my ' disc' in the pocket as a result of my one-time utterance of words in mother tongue that day. It was a stone, a rather big one for a class seven kid already with a big head to carry around. But who cared though. All they wanted is for us to have perfect English pronunciation and adhere to the colonizer's language while shelving our mothers' languages at home. It was always a bother why my teachers could speak in Kisii within themselves while treating us like demons whenever we tried. You should have been there every evening; the teacher on duty calling out the names of anyone who had touched the disc that day and raining on them with whips as if they were exorcising evil spirits. Someone pointed this out to me earlier this week, comparing it to an alternate universe situation where we should have English kids beaten at the Royal Palace for speaking the Queen's language, rather than working their tongues out in practising the language of the great conquerors of Luhya land who brought Ugali civilization and the gospel of the great Jehovah Wanyonyi. I will, however, pin this conversation up for another day, as that is not the highlight of this story today. The problem I have with writing is that once my fingers start clicking away on the keyboard, I get so excited by the endless thoughts and ideas running through my head and feel like I cannot manage to stick to the initial one. Am I not the curator of my space? What wrong is there if I start this post with a discussion into pan-Africanism and end up talking about a time when I was furiously chased by a hen? Si you guys come to this space to listen to me beat stories? But no, I will keep it grounded. I will moderate today and as a good moderator, I shall insist we stick to the topic for once. This is a good panel, we shall beat stories, laugh and I stay focused. Let me line up the puns. Alright, so where were we. Class seven, disc, mother tongue. Oh yeah. If it so happened that you went home without punishment for holding the disc at any point through your day, the last person in possession takes it home till the next day when you all shall receive your beating. Woe unto you if it so happened that the day fell on a Friday. It meant through the weekend all you had to think about was the caning that will be the grand opening of your new week. There would, therefore, be no need to make new week resolutions as you had already been screwed before you had been screwed. On very rare occasions, you could be lucky to start afresh the next Monday with a new teacher on duty who would take the courtesy of forgiving the previous week's delinquents. It so happened that the day I carried that heavy stone home was the eve to the annual ASK show in Kisii stadium. All the way home, I begged Jesus to come to my rescue. "Lord, remember that time when Satan made jokes that you turn stones into bread. I think now would be a really good time to show him who's boss."
I looked up to the mountain tops, where does my help come from? All I could see were bigger stones there.
So I gave up and walked home. The only genius idea I had was to convince my very strict father to sponsor me to the showground the next day and force the school to start afresh with a new disc. I braced myself, walked home, showered and did my homework before requesting my dear mother to phone him (I can't tell you why all that preparation was necessary). Almost felt like I was being a good boy for Santa. My old man surprisingly took it well and sent the required amount for transportation costs. I had no idea I required an entrance fee, which was at 100Ksh at that time. Getting into the showground with a couple of other classmates, I had to jump over the fence. On landing, I was not so lucky as there was a guard stationed right behind that part of the fence with a baton. At 12 years of age, there was no way I was going to escape a caning in school, spend fare in a rickety matatu so that I could go to receive a beating from a guard. That's a very expensive way of looking for trouble. So I told the guard to go and kiss a porcupine’s ass and I took off into the crowd. I walked around the showground and was amazed by what was on display. My naive self found a tent with a 'mermaid' and was scared shitless for the next couple of weeks after I saw it. Other tents were displaying a headless man, a head without a body and the world's shortest woman. I did not want to push my luck with these tents because I did not have enough money to buy me some diapers for the scare that would rock my rectum. I hoped on a merry-go-round and puked like an active volcano. I am yet to go back up there in my adult life. Apparently, the world's second strongest man was a Kenyan and was displaying his prowess too. A car ran over his stomach, he pulled a truck with his bare teeth and a rope and lifted a crate of a soda with his lips. I didn't quite understand how his strength extended from his muscles to unconnected body parts like the mouth and teeth.

But I had one conclusion for sure, this was not a man you allow to marry your daughter.

With all the wild fun I had that many years ago, it is therefore not a surprise when I suggested to the other half of me to accompany me to the Nairobi ASK showground this year. The fare from town was 100 shillings which was not a problem. The entry fee was however 300 shillings, and this was quite a bother to me. I mean, what? It’s not like they were feeding me in there. I resisted the urge to jump over the fence and let my fine lady pay for her entry fee. The guards at that place looked like they were just straight from a nasty divorce and I didn't want them to vent their anger on my head with those heavy rungus they were carrying. Also, I am trying very much to pump my personality and become a well-respected model. Role model that is, not the one where I have to catwalk (Hi Lewis). If you didn't attend the Show this year, then you really need to tell me where you spent your day. Everything was an absolute bomb. From the food carts, the chicken smokies that I ate for the first time ever, the rabbit choma (What cruel human slaughters a rabbit though), the small SGR model that was on display and several agrotech innovations. My best attractions from years ago were also present; the mermaid, the head without a body and the shortest woman in the world. I went in to see the mermaid and I wasn't as scared as I last was. Someone's daughter did not, however, have the guts. She insisted that she was too old to start looking for diapers, so I had her stay outside the tent. I will still never step foot in the head without body tent. It may sound childish to you, but I have to remind you that I come from Bondonya clan right at the feet of Sameta Hill in the great Kisii County.

Headless men, talking heads attached to no body and other bizarre occurrences will sound like a joke to you until you meet one in the dark at 10pm.

It is at that point you will realize why KICC is in the shape of a donkey's mjuols. I did not get to go up the merry-go-round because I felt like I would be nauseated and puke on the fresh pair of kicks I had acquired the previous day. They also did not let me go into the bouncing castle, saying something about the age limit. I tried to convince them I did not have an ID, but my 3 strands of beard betrayed me. We took a few photos with the madam and laughed like kids from 10 years ago. The innocence of childhood was triggered in me and for a moment I looked away from the struggles of adulthood.
Here we were, walking around believing in mermaids, savouring the moment, eating cotton candy and laughing like we were a couple of primary school kids.
Sitting in a matatu that evening watching the sun set on Nairobi as we found our way back home, I realized I was at the happiest moment of my life. I was receiving joy and radiating love; my cheeks were glowing, and my heart was at home and in peace. I was also wearing clean socks and boxers, but that is not the point here. It is the little things in life that bring out happiness, and also the fact that you can speak Kisii without a heavy stone landing in your pocket in the name of a disc.

content: Alright. So, I was just right about now getting cosy in my digz, binge-watching The Hobbit since I am on a break from the drudgery that is Kenyan campus life, when it hit me that today is the last day of November. Through the year I have maintained a streak of writing a post every month in these streets for you folks, and it would be a shame if I did not aspire to maintain that. As summer bunny Solomon Jesse (Resident book reviewer) would say, “dedication over motivation”. Maybe someday when you all decide to pay me for beating stories in this space, I will seek to write weekly. Haisuru, I will lighten up my travel category this time with a somewhat detailed tale of my recent short sojourn at the Republic of South Africa. Acquiring a stamp of approval in the name of a Visa from the South African High Commission is a very daunting and extremely draining process, or at least that’s what it proved to be for me. In a continent where we are all referred to as a shithole, we ought to be tolerant to each other with matters immigration. Kenya offers all South Africans Visas on arrival though South Africa requires that all Kenyans make an application that takes at least a week to be reviewed before issuance (or rejection thereof). I don’t see the logic. Or maybe there are levels to this thing. In this continent, we are all equal shitholes, but it seems there are those who are more equal than the rest. They do not accept just anything that is dumped in them. Na-ah. They are those ones that require about three flashes for shit from other parts to go through. They are like those toilets where if it’s a bad day for you, your guava filled defecation will not flow with the water. It will float and stare back at you, leaving you in a state of panic as on the other side of the door stands your girlfriend’s dad. It’s just a bad day. And a bad day it was for me. Five thousand Kenyan Shillings, a Yellow fever vaccination, a completed application form in black ink, the feathers of a dodo, confirmed flight bookings, the blood of a cow with green tits and valid bank statements is all I required to get things going. Sounds easy. Since I was to travel with the galdem, I decided we should all make our Visa application at the same time and synchronize the process. Her passport was not yet out from Nyayo house, and that’s a whole other blogpost of frustrations that I will pen another time. When her passport was finally processed, we barely had a week to make our application in time for our travel arrangements. This triggered the hustle for gathering documentation. Neither of us have bank statements with sufficient funds hence we had to move around trying to convince our paranoid African parents that their statements were not being offered to the Illuminati. (I wonder what exactly they mean sufficient funds, because I have been able to survive in this pad for the past week with ugali nyanya and the strength provided by the blood of the lamb). The first day submitting our application saw us waste valuable Kenyan currency in fare. We were turned back and told to top our documents with confirmed hotel bookings for all the days we intended to stay in the South. “Ooh, and darlings, you do not need those hideous bank statements because everything is catered for you by your South African host.” Hallelujah praise the Lord. We discard the bank statements, whoosh home, request our hotel bookings be sent over and then wake up the next day all the way from the furthest corners of Thika road to Westlands and make our application again. “The Visa is processed between 5-8 business days...,” so reads their website. We are here, on a Friday, exactly five business days to our projected travel date and we can only request our ancestors to expedite this process. During this period, I am juggling between CATs, lab practicals and my Drive show on radio. My body is about to burst from all these responsibilities. As we walk out of the application centre with our receipts waiting eagerly for the collection day, we chant “It is well”. It wasn’t. On the eve of collection, I receive a text I consider malicious, full of typos, requesting me to avail my tired Kenyan bottoms to the South African High Commission all the way in Lenana Road to submit bank statements. These guys are nut jobs. First you tell me you need them, then you say you don’t, now you need them? Sounds like the relationship between me and Kenyan education. I am pissed. Naturally, since our applications were similar, I expected that the galdem had received the texts also. Turns out I was wrong. We speculated that either they were to text her the next day, or somehow her Meru ancestors had worked overtime for her application to go through, while that old geezer Sakawa was playing darts with my other Kisii ancestors. So, I am at Lenana Road, the gateman is Kisii, and he chats me up asking why I am there. I show him the text and he request to see the bank statements before he gives me a pass. I say I don’t have them, and he goes, “No, Shit?!” Okay, actually he doesn’t but it would have been awesome if he did. On second thought, that would sound really horrible in Kisii. Anyways, I tell him I don’t need bank statements and I am here to explain the same. I leave my phone at the gate with galdem because I might be trying to hack the High Commission with my cheap android and discover the cause of xenophobic attacks in their country. Hey, I’m kidding. Embassies and commissions world over require that phones be left at the gate so I’m down with that. I’m going to paint a picture of the South African High Commission so that you guys can see my ordeal clearly. Okay, here we go. The offices are on the second floor of the building, there is a guard at the lift on the ground floor who requests to see your gate pass before allowing you to proceed. On alighting the second floor is another guard who then escorts or points you to the room. When I get here, there is a lady and man behind a glass screen (Like those at banking halls) and I immediately realise they are Kenyan. A sigh of relief as I stand up to be served, hoping my fellow Kenyans will help me out. Boy, was I wrong! “Haro, how may I heop you?” “ Okay, I am to travel tomorrow to South Africa but I received a text to submit bank statements first” “And where are they” “Hehe, thing is, I was told at the application center that you don’t need them” “Okay, then I am telling you that we need them” “ Who is your host” “ A European company that has offices in South Africa” “ Then tell them to submit their statements or you submit yours” “ Thing is, I do not have sufficient bank statements personally, and I do not think they can give me their bank statements.” “ Then I cannot heop you” Help goddamit, just say Help. I was getting agitated at his insistence on being adamant and I could see he clearly was getting agitated. So, I walk out, back to the gate and make a phone call to my hosts. They insist that they are a big corporation with roots allover more than 10 countries and it would be virtually impossible to trust a young man from the unknown Sameta hill in Kisii county with their bank statements. Its like telling Coca-Cola or Safaricom to provide someone they have never met with bank statements. So, I request them to call the High Commission before I am told again to go hug a porcupine. They say fine. I head back inside and the ninja looks confused to sight my big head in the premises. “Wewe si nimekuambia utoke hapa?” “Hapana, it’s not like that, ni vile you don’t want to understand my situation” “ Tell the host to send their bank statement or you bring yours as an employee of your company” “First of all, my company is a startup, I don’t have a pay slip. Secondly, my hosts have been trying to call you guys, but you are not picking their calls” “We are not picking any calls boss. Leave this premises or risk rejection for your Visa.” I get angry. This is 8th November, my travel date is the morning of 9th and the dude doesn’t seem like he gave a rat’s ass about my arrangements. In my desperation, I lodge a heated argument with him. Him shouting behind the glass, me shouting on the other side. Before I know it, the guard manning the floor’s lift taps my shoulder. “Boss, toa huyo mtu hapa!!” The guy yells angrily behind the glass as I am escorted out like a disorderly drunk. The other citizens sited on the waiting benches stare at me, a misplaced agitated 20year old in their eyes, and I feel like barking at their faces. Indeed, men are dogs if given a chance. I am literally given a shove into the elevator by the guard and as it descends to the ground floor, I look up and see my South African dream rise up in smoke.

content:
“Do you guys know which terminal you are supposed to go to?” Queries my dad from the driver’s seat as we approach the security check point off Mombasa road getting to JKIA.

“What’s a terminal?” I ask naively.
He chuckles softly, turns to look at me and wipes the grin after he realizes that I am serious.


The closest I had been to an airport to this point is back in my form two at Entebbe in Uganda. My old man had just been thoroughly disappointed after expecting a fat cheque from work and having already pre-planned a flight for us back to Kenya at the end of our short holidaying period in the buzzling city of Kampala. He decided to take us to the airport and point to us like he was God and were Moses, “There is the promised flight, you can see it but you shall never board it”

It was therefore somewhat of an exciting moment that the person seeing me and the galdem off on both of our first flights was the man who first showed me the entrails of an airport.
“I’m very sure it’s somewhere here in the boarding pass.” I chyme as I check my mail for the specifics, in time to alighting from the car for the security screening.

xxxxxxxxxx
The last time I left you burgers, I had no hopes of getting me a Visa after being kicked out of the South African High Commission here. After that torturous purgatory-like ordeal, I walk out of their gates muttering and cursing under my breath, wishing that the fool is struck by bouts of unending diarrhea or someone laces the tissue paper he uses with pepper. The Kisii guard at the gate requests me for a handout and in my frustrated state, I really care less about money. I whip out a crisp note and give it to him, walk out and order a cab. Me and the galdem head out to the Visa application center at Westlands to check whether her Visa is out.

 

Her Visa was out fam. Same documentation with mine, but she got hers and I hadn’t. No bank statements but she got hers and I hadn’t. It is the evening of November 8th, we are to travel together on the morning of 9th, she got hers and I hadn’t. I phone my South African host company and update them of the events and they promise to send in their bank statements because they really had no other option. Plus, there was no way I was going to miss my first time on a flight, I’d freaking walk all the way to Zimbabwe and take a flight from their if so be it. Inevitably, we had to reschedule the flight to the next week because there was no way those burgers were going to receive the bank statements and hand me my Visa on the same day. Haisuru. Heading out to Waiyaki way to catch a mat home, a plane passes just right above Safaricom House, I clench my teeth and raise my fist up and shake it in anger. I feel like the gods are having a laugh at my miserable ass. I ask galdem what she thinks we shall do if my Visa doesn’t come out at all.


"Maybe you should go solo and represent us well." I say.
"If yours isn’t processed I won’t go either, there’s no point." She says back.
     Love is foolish you guys.

      xxxxxxxx


Monday 12th November, My Visa is out, and I am rushing around like an adolescent on Viagra. I head to pick it from Westie, pick a suitcase from Kiambu, book our flights for the next morning of 13th, get excited, see my barber all the way in Kangundo road for a decent cut. For some reason, the flight bookings were not going through on any airline that night. 3am and we didn’t have a booking yet, the credit card kept getting rejected. Swinging between excitement and anxiety, my feelings were allover the place. We were so close, yet so far. It was as late as 5am when the booking went through. I was unable to catch sleep for the remaining time, our flight was scheduled for the afternoon and I couldn’t believe it that the evening of the same day I would be in Johannesburg ready for my connecting flight to Capetown. Yaani me, the grandson of Chang’aa sellers in the village of Bondonya at the foot of Sameta Hill. Somebody slap the fuck out of me so I can wake up from this absolute dream.

I phone my old man and convince him to drop us off because Matiang’i had scared matatus off their job. I estimated cab fare from Thika road all the way to JKIA could be enough to open a small kindergarten for all the kids named Jayden in my neighborhood. We chill at the Java House in JKIA before time, having absolutely no idea that there is a difference between boarding time and departure time and almost missed boarding as we were the last ones in. We took our sweet time licking the sauce off our plates with the costly Java fries. (It is at this point I should mention that the attendants at JKIA Java are lazy, extremely racist Kenyans, and just purely arrogant. But this is a blogpost for another time also.)- Or they just didn’t like my big head, you never know with its weird shape the types of emotions it elicits.


Take-off is extremely amazing. His verses in the Migos songs always hit the spot. Ooh, it’s the flight. Sorry. I meant, the plane taking off is an out of this world feeling because well, you’re being lifted off the surface of the world. As the engine roared and the wings heaved, I could not believe what was about to happen. Something extremely monumental and historic in my life, I could finally get all those memes about flying from that day onwards! Early on, I had passed the fat blokes at the business class, all of them with rich smirks and probably going down south to see ‘why the office in Gauteng was not churning the right numbers.’ I looked at them, with such huge leg room , a very comfortable seat with better leaning capabilities than the economy class and them big-ass headphones. Lucky bastards! I didn’t care much though, I wanted to be on a plane and I was. The world from way up looks so insignificant, everything looks like a dot on a piece of paper, whether it be a very big city or a huge ego. I imagined there are people having lotsa pressure with their lives, frustrations, sadness because some douchebag decided to wrong them, tribalism because they think they are better than the next man who only just speaks a different language. I looked up from my window into the heavens, saw the vast expanse of the universe and realized how the things we think are too important are very trivial to the structure of the universe we live in. After the deep thoughts I decide to take a shit on the moving plane at that altitude because that’s a cloud nine experience. Haha.

O.R Tambo International Airport in Johannesburg is the biggest and busiest airport you can be at on this side of the planet. It’s toilets are extremely clean and they have tissue paper for days. Landing at around 6:20pm and without something to do till around 8pm when boarding to Cape town, I decided to check on my folks on WhatsApp. There was no open Wi-Fi so I decided to roam with the 50MB that I had on my Saf line. Don’t try it ever fam. Only one text managed to come through. Dear Customer, your data balance is below 2MB. My host for the first night at the University of Capetown had informed me that there would be Wi-Fi at Cape Town International Airport and so I stayed calm till then, when I’d be able to contact him for directions and also inform people I had arrived.

Finally landing at Cape town, we are happy and thankful. Armed with some thousands of Kenyan shillings in cash and some more on my debit card, we headed out to look for an exchange bureau in the airport. Ha, Shaka.11pm at Cape town International Airport, no exchange bureau is open, and we don’t have a cent in South African Rand. I say it’s okay. We can always change the next day. So I fish out my phone to text my host on and inform him of my arrival so they can facilitate my taxi until I can be able to change my currency.


No open Wi-Fi network. Wow, Okay, don’t panic. Not much people at the airport at this point but don’t panic. No way to know how you’re supposed to call your host or even inform them of your predicament, but don’t panic.

Two 20-year-olds, a useless Kenyan sim-card, no Wi-Fi, Useless Kenyan currency at this point and completely clueless on how to proceed. Ayam tired and stranded in a foreign country. I sigh, sit down and close my eyes. I flashback to my ordeal at the High Commission.

You cannot cheat death twice.

title: Book Review — East of Eden
creator: Solomonjesse

content:
Dear John,

Thank you. Thank you for the free trip to California. For taking your precious time to write me a 300 page guide on Salinas Valley, King City and even once, New York (remember that time when you told me of the train carrying vegetables in ice boxes cutting across different states?).

In a book’s world, the author is a demigod with the power to create, build or snuff out characters at will.

Thank you for showing me your creations. For giving me such an explicit narration of how the fickle lives of your creatures intertwined – sometimes in the simplest of ways. Thank you for Cyrus Trask, and his wife who killed herself (Up to now, I am still not sure if one can justify that type of suicide). Thank you for the methodical and meticulous molding of Adam Trask and his brother Charles – they were to me like Jade dynasty statues, sold off at those art events where every bidder has tattoos on their faces and arm muscles and a tiger in golden chains lying at their feet. And a monkey. You can never forget the monkey – Yakuza style aye?

John, of late I zone out a lot: during conversations, on walks home in the evenings, while washing my hands after visiting the offices for a short call… I space out so much, it’s almost scary. My mind escapes my body unannounced. It flies over streams, rivers, mountains, continents, and oceans to Salinas.

Most of the time, its winter over there.
I don’t know why.

I see a lot of snowflakes falling quietly from the dull grey sky and secretly piling up on the brown earth, covering the purple azalea that had sprung up in the previous autumn. John, I see Sam Hamilton a lot. Sometimes I think it’s because he was Irish, but then again, it could be because I greatly admired his subtle intelligence and generosity. It’s rare to find an intellectual who is genuinely generous I think. There is a way in which you created an air around him that sweeps me head over heels.

It’s not only Sam that I see at such times. I see Adam Trask too. I pity him greatly for his gullibility in love. I feel deep remorse for his trial. For creating a Garden of Eden for a wife who did not even love him. It disheartens that he is so blindly in love with Catherine Amesbury that he fails to see the stark flaws in her.

Maybe that is how we all are when we fall in love with someone. We project the picture of what we want our ideal lover to be on them and forget that they are equally human, sometimes with spots so dark we would be scared to draw any closer.

I got a question for you, John.

Do emotional monsters actually exist?

Did you conjure Catherine Amesbury as a fictitious character, or have you, in your lifetime, come across such an evil, heinous being. It felt so... inhuman, for one to do such things as she for the sake of personal gain.

That aside, I fell in love with your narration - so soothing - it pairs well with espresso. It was a complex story, yes, but you made it work. I think it was ingenious how the Trask and the Hamilton family existed in perpetual hatred for each other yet in so many ways, they were each other's closest of friends. How would Adam Trask, for example, have come back to his senses were it not for the friendly pelting of Sam Hamilton? Such was a true friendship, a deep, brotherly kinship that left me in awe. You, John, breathed life into your story and like an ever-present companion, made them real. The idea of inter-generational curses. The essence of human life?

Is it true that at the end of our lives, we will stop and look back at all our deeds in

black

and

white?

That we will think of our actions as good or bad? That as we lay in our deathbed, we will wonder if the people we leave behind will think us of good or bad people. In a world where the greys wax more and more over the blacks and whites, this is an extremely scary concept.

Best,
S.

creator: Richard

'As it was in the beginning, so it is now, and evermore shall be.'.

This third one though. This I did not want to end. 
Amazing piece.👌

comments.author: Sonnie

content:
There is a vast difference between waking up in the noisy, smoke ridden, traffic plagued, hawker heckling, mare mare neighbourhoods of Nairobi and the ambience of the cool, warm, sunny morning – filled with the humming of exotic birds – that I woke up to in my room at the University of Cape Town (UCT).

You wake up at the crack of dawn at Cape Town whereas you survive the night in Nairobi.

The cocks that usually crow in the morning at my place sound exhausted and I am sure they are plotting to abdicate their role and let the goats take over giving the morning shout-outs like a certain Deejay Khalid.

I am not saying this out of spite or disrespect for my own hometown. I think you would pen the same description if you woke up, drew your curtains and were faced with the Table Mountains, a wonder of the world. Campus shuttles plodded the streets below, picking up students and dropping them off. Uber drivers roamed about waiting for those requiring extra comfort to access their classes. Just wow. That is what I call arrogant privilege. I imagined myself riding an uber in the streets of Kenyatta University heading to sit for my retake.

Wild, fam.

Previously on Survivor…

Last time we met, Galdems and I was stranded at Cape Town International Airport with no means whatsoever of getting to UCT. After a few minutes of meditation, I remembered I had kitu a hundred shillings in my Mpesa. I buy airtime, call my host and quickly inform him of my predicament because the roaming rates wouldn’t allow for longer talks. A wise man, he orders a cab, calls me back with the plate numbers of our driver and his name. As for currency exchange, the hosts were travelling to Kenya sooner than us and therefore needed the Kenyan cash we had and we their Rands. Easy work.

Relieved, we step out of the airport and head to our pick-up point. The first thing I do is deeply inhale the Cape Town air and I was immediately shook to find no smell of mutura in the atmosphere. Such arrogant sophistication. I thought these guys were big on diversity? Henwei.

It is either Kenyan taxi drivers have utter disregard for the navigation tools of their apps or we are years behind Cape Town drivers in discipline. There are no calls with questions like “Uko wapi? Eeh? Sikupati vizuri…ati Nation Centre?...gani?...Si usimame side ya KFC ninatoka na Kimathi street.” There is no “karibu boss, unaenda wapi?” Because in Capetown, the maps make sense. Just sit, connect your bluetooth, jam to Mpongo Love as the cab cuts across the first-world highways and the navigator guides the driver to your desired destination.. I thought, “damn I could just throw away my passport and live here”. This driver dude even stopped at a red light when it was midnight and zero cars on the roads.

If he was in Kenya, catch him like a rat on the haiweei.

The population in Cape Town is predominantly white, or at least it’s what my quack analytics picked up. (I am not aware of the factual figures but I strongly suspect Quack Analytica was pretty on point). Haidhuru. The roads are extremely clean, I bet the germs in their streets even say the grace and wash their hands before feasting. Fancy glass houses, well-manicured lawns, balcony swimming pools and electric gates were sending shock waves through my village consciousness. Tour buses, cable cars to the mountain tops, efficient shuttle services and beach houses with helipads? Did we just get to Canaan before my mans Rao?

We had our first experience at McDonalds, which is just another KFC with a fancy name. We however still ate to our fill, which we indeed ought to have because our first cold dish of racism was boiling in the kitchen.

On that second day, we went trotting up Newlands Forest in Table Mountain National park with our UCT hosts. We took strolls into places even they had not been because what’s more fun than getting lost in a forest, right? I remembered what I had seen happen in the ‘Wrong Turn’ movies and decided to put on some music to ease tension. Some white dudes were jogging and cycling (who the hell cycles up a mountain?) or walking dogs up the forest. Most of the dogs were as big as calves, looked very mature and composed and walked with a springy gait that suggested they ate three course meals and had butlers with names like ‘Clifford’ or Alfred’.

As we hiked, we also did a little dancing to the music beaming from my phone. Two of the dogs felt like they were being provoked and started barking. All of us panicked, like the Africans we are, and started running. Those beasts decide they like black meat, open their mouths wide and start chasing.

 
            “Stop fucking running!!” shouted the white dog-owner.


I stop running because the barking is a bit far off, turn back and realize galdem is being chased in circles. She is still clutching to her handbag though, despite her screams and calls for help as she hopelessly tries to outrun the enraged canines. What exactly do women carry in those bags that they have to hold on tightly to? I guessed it could be printed screenshots to be unleashed for when they are needed.

“I said stop running, what the fuck is wrong with you?” the white dude shouted again at which point we realized he was directing that to us and not to his dogs.

Wow.

All along, this guy had been hurling insults and ranking us lower than his animals. All along, us acting on our gut instincts, running away from his dogs and reacting to adrenaline was fucking wrong, more than his dogs randomly going after us.

The rest of us were now standing at a safe distance, attention shifted to her and her bag of screenshots, like sunflower to the sun. When she finally stopped, she was exhausted, scared to the bone and angry to the core. But ironically not angrier than the dog owner who should have in the first place adhered to the rules of the National park and put his dogs on a leash. Or would he have preferred having us on a leash?

            “Don’t be fucking idiots !”

The dude sized us and hissed like the colonialist he was before he walked off. At that point, I wished Dedan Kimathi was hibernating somewhere in the forest with the Mau Mau crew so he’d come and psyche me. I was angered at that ‘subtle’ show of racism from someone who was obviously the one on the wrong side of the law. I ashamedly brushed it off because we were still lost in the forest and had to find our way out in time for us to move from UCT to our hotel in Century City.

Yezzzz. A hotel. In Century City. A place on the other side of the Teibal Mountains (Thats how us South Efricans say it) where we were to participate in a tech startup/investor conference dubbed AfricArena on behalf of our startup company, Medbit

As we were heading to the hotel from our botched excursion, I looked at my galdems and then at the sun setting behind the table mountain. All that came out of my mouth was:

“ That is the most beautiful thing I have ever seen in my life”

The next day on a Citywide tour by our generous hosts at AfricArena, me and the galdems witnessed the sun setting on the Atlantic Ocean.

“Screw the mountains, this is the most beautiful scene of my life.”

Repeat scene the next day when I went to the Muizenberg beach.
And the next when we went up the top of Table mountains; magnificent view of the city tucked safely at the bottom.
And when I saw a Ferrari in the dealership store.
Or when I saw Robben island from across the ocean

Like a four-year-old child at the Show.

Soon I realized almost everything I saw in Capetown was ‘more beautiful than the last’ and so I reverted from “That is the most beautiful thing I have ever seen in my life” to “Ooh shit, look at that absolute beauty.” Sounded more touristy and I didn’t have to look like a stereotypical African villager every type I opened my mouth. Cape Town changes you fam.

Our gracious hosts treated us to a dinner at the shores of the Atlantic Ocean where we had wine and chatted with big money from all over the globe. The expensive glass houses with helipads right by the beach fascinated me until it hit me that all the people I saw accessing them were white. I noted that the people serving tables at the restaurants/bars were always all black people while the manager and the guy at the counter were mostly white.

Was this by chance or was it apartheid and institutionalized racism still playing out years after Africans had claimed independence? (Yes we are talking about this.)

We were now at the last day of the conference and our hotel accommodation had automatically expired. Since we were touristy, we had intended to extend our stay in Cape Town and therefore began our search for a cheap Airbnb hosting at 6pm. There was no way we were going to afford accommodation at the same five-star hotel, located in one of the most expensive parts of Cape Town. Me and galdem sat right outside the Conference Centre where the Wi-Fi was still accessible because the halls were being closed for the evening. I still only had my Kenyan simcards like the fool I was – three days into my stay in South Africa – because we had been relying on Wi-Fi the whole time we were there. The cash I had was only an approximation of cab fare to wherever my hosting would be, where I planned to withdraw what I had in my debit card. The Math was exact fam. The guard at the conference Centre requests to know why we are still outside the halls, and we lie that our Uber is on the way as I rush to create my profile and book for accommodation. Ten minutes later, the host, a white woman, declines my request for accommodation citing that she is not around at the time.

Strange. How does one have their place up open for accommodation on Airbnb and still claim not to be around?

Racism.

It’s getting dangerously dark, we have no accommodation, money or food. The guard is clearly agitated at our claim to be waiting for an uber thirty minutes later. Meanwhile, I’m still looking at the profile of this white mama who has declined my request for no reason.

Racism.

I ask for directions from a guy leaving the conference, to direct us to the nearest mall where I would be able to purchase an MTN line for internet as I place another Airbnb request. At the mall, I buy my line, hungry, angry and getting broke real fast, I place another request which gets declined without reason offered by yet another white couple.

Racism.

My phone’s charge is getting to 5%, galdem’s phone is dead, the mall is closing up because it is super late, and we have no idea where we are going to sleep.

Fam, It was just like my high-school principal David Gideon Kariuki used to say: As it was in the beginning, so it is now, and evermore shall be.

I sit on a bench and request the gods to come through for me.

Suddenly Cape Town was not so exciting for me. Suddenly, the allure of being in a foreign land wasn’t so exciting for us. The galdem was close to tears, a very frustrated human being. I was feeling hopeless, especially reminiscent of the fact that I had struggled to get to these lands and here I was struggling to survive the experience as well.

My phone beeps, it’s my editor on this blog. He suggests to us that we google for the cheapest hotels and lodgings nearest to us. Reluctantly, we do so and manage to find some, in places around Cape Town whose names we couldn’t pronounce. The name Cape Town was itself beginning to taste bitter in my Kenyan mouth. I longed for Nairobi, for mahindi choma, for humans who could accommodate me in their house, for humans who don’t care about skin color. I did not play Mpongo Love in the cab on our way to the hotel that night.

Because at that point, Cape Town did not deserve any love from me.

title: Hello world!
creator: Richard
